Title: Fire and Rescue Dept bids farewell to veteran officer Ahmad Izram
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: After more than three decades in uniform, Fire and Rescue Department deputy director-general (Operations) Datuk Ahmad Izram Osman bade farewell to the service today, leaving behind a decorated legacy marked by operational leadership, innovation and hopes for a stronger, more technologically advanced department.
Ahmad Izram, who officially retires on May 24 after 34 years and five months in service, said he hoped the department would continue progressing and expanding key innovations, particularly the Command, Control, Communication, Computer and Intelligence (C4i) system nationwide.
He said the system, currently focused on the Klang Valley, had significantly improved operational efficiency and real-time emergency response management.
"We hope the government will continue supporting us in expanding the programme throughout Malaysia because the system is very good.
"Everything is computerised. We can monitor deployment time, body cameras, instructions from the operations centre and allow many parties to observe and provide advice during operations," he said when met after the ceremony late yesterday evening.
Ahmad Izram also expressed hope that the department would continue advancing beyond its current achievements under future leadership.
"I hope the department will progress further than where it is now," he said, while urging his successor to continue the work and direction set by the department's top leadership.
Earlier, the Fire and Rescue Department held a full ceremonial parade at its headquarters here to honour Ahmad Izram's retirement, with the event attended by director-general Datuk Seri Nor Hisham Mohammad, senior management, state directors and officers.
Over the years, Ahmad Izram held several strategic posts, including Penang Fire and Rescue Department director, director of the Research and Planning Division, Fire Investigation Division, Fire Safety Division and Fire Operations and Rescue Division before being appointed deputy director-general (Development) and later deputy director-general (Operations) in April 2024.
Among his notable contributions were his involvement in forest fire operations in South Sumatra, Indonesia in 1997, coordinating Ops Kemarau in Sabah and Sarawak, and representing Malaysia as a forest firefighting technical expert in the ASEAN Transboundary Haze Committee.
The department also credited Ahmad Izram for helping develop the C4i system and strengthening the implementation of Fire Service Fees to improve service delivery efficiency.
Holding a Master's degree in Fire Safety Engineering from the University of Western Sydney and another in Emergency Response Planning from Universiti Putra Malaysia, Ahmad Izram also underwent extensive international firefighting and hazardous materials training in the United States, Australia and Japan throughout his career.
He was also deeply involved in welfare and technical committees, including serving as president of the department's welfare association and as coordinator for the inquest involving the late firefighter Muhammad Adib Mohd Kassim.
Reflecting on his long career, Ahmad Izram said he had no regrets and was proud of the culture of innovation fostered within the department.
"We not only worked, we innovated. The top management allowed us to be creative and many of those innovations were accepted," he said.
Nor Hisham described Ahmad Izram as a towering figure who had dedicated his life to strengthening the organisation and public service throughout his career, which began on Jan 13, 1992.
"I had pleasure of working under the supervision of Ahmad Izram before I was promoted to helm the department.
"He was among the key individuals who shaped and strengthened the department's fire investigation framework during a major transformation era within the Fire and Rescue Department," he said adding that he learnt a lot from him.
He said Ahmad Izram's attention to detail enabled him to play a major role in developing policies and systems that continue benefiting the department today.
"One of his lasting legacies was helping establish and strengthen the Fire Service Fees mechanism, which now contributes towards the welfare fund for firefighters and station maintenance.
"His commitment was unquestionable. Even during major emergencies, he would immediately respond and continue carrying out his responsibilities, setting an example for future generations of firefighters," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pentagon publishes new UFO records including reports of 'green orbs' and discs
Article:
WSHINGTON: The US Defence Department on Friday released a second batch of  previously classified files on alleged UFO sightings, including references to people reporting unexplained green orbs, discs and fireballs.
At the order of President Donald Trump, the first batch was made public on May 8. He is the latest president to release US government reports on unidentified flying objects, a disclosure process that began in the late 1970s.
Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth said the documents, photos and videos of what are officially known as "unidentified anomalous phenomena" had long fuelled speculation.
"It's time the American people see it for themselves," he said in a statement.
One of the 222 files released on Friday contains 116 pages of documentation relating to a series of reported sightings and investigations in a top secret facility in Sandia, New Mexico, from 1948 to 1950.
"This file contains 209 sightings of 'green orbs', 'discs', and 'fireballs' reported near the military base," the Defence Department said.
Experts said the first batch contained new videos of known sightings but gave no conclusive evidence of alien technology or extraterrestrial life. --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Bakery's order-calling system leaves customers stressed and impressed [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A popular bakery has gone viral for its remarkably orderly system that managed to turn a peak-hour rush into a display of peak efficiency.
The video captures rows of bakery boxes meticulously lined up as a worker distributes them by loudly and clearly announcing order numbers with rhythmic precision.
Rather than the usual frantic scramble seen at popular eateries, the crowd appeared calm and entertained by the worker's thorough and authoritative distribution style.
One netizen noted that the worker's delivery was so intense it felt more like a grand announcement than a simple bread pickup.
Another user joked that the level of suspense created by the worker made them feel like they were waiting for their name to be called at a graduation ceremony.
"I've never been so stressed and impressed at the same time; it's like she's hosting a live show," commented one amused viewer.

One social media user suggested the worker should be given a raise for doubling as a professional announcer and crowd controller.
"She doesn't just call your number, she validates your entire purchase with that energy," quipped another netizen.
The viral clip has sparked a wave of positive feedback, with many commending the bakery for finding a way to make waiting in line an enjoyable and orderly experience.
